sound_type: loud wing slap on takeoff -- sharp percussive clapping of wings above the body during display flights and alarm takeoffs; third mechanical sound in the engine; E=4
ecosystem: Urban cliffs -- buildings, bridges, overpasses, parking garages. Any vertical structure mimicking Mediterranean cliff faces. World's most successful urban bird. Feral populations on every inhabited continent. Estimated 260 million global feral population.
season: Year-round resident. Year-round breeding in urban environments. Wing clap displays and alarm flights year-round.
family: Columbidae (4th and final entry. FOURTH AND FINAL COLUMBIDAE. Completes the quartet 149-152.)

coordinates.description: Rock Pigeons are ubiquitous in urban Seattle -- downtown buildings, bridges, overpasses, parking garages, waterfront structures. Every vertical surface is a potential cliff face.

taxonomy_note.description: FOURTH and FINAL Columbidae entry. Completes the quartet. Feral descendant of domesticated stock. Domesticated ~5,000-10,000 years ago in the Middle East. Transported globally by humans. Urban cliff substitute habitat. Wing clap focus: mechanical sound, not vocal. THIRD mechanical sound in engine.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Columbidae: fourth and final entry. Band-tailed (149) + Mourning Dove (150) + Collared-Dove (151) + Rock Pigeon (152). Quartet complete.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Columba from Latin 'columba' (dove, pigeon). livia from Latin 'livid' (blue-gray) -- referring to the wild-type plumage color.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Columba -- first entry. The 'typical pigeons' -- Old World genus. The ancestral pigeon genus.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Fourth and final Columbidae. Feral. Mechanical sound focus. Completes native-to-feral progression: native forest (149) > native open (150) > invasive (151) > feral (152).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Wing clap: broadband 200-5000 Hz. Not tonal. Percussive impulse. Coo (secondary): 300-600 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Wing clap: broadband energy, peak percussive impact at 500-2000 Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Wing clap: 200-5000 Hz broadband. This is a mechanical, percussive sound -- not a vocalization.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual clap: 0.01-0.03 seconds. Display sequence: 3-8 claps over 1-3 seconds. Flock takeoff: hundreds of claps in 2-5 second burst.
song_metrics.note_structure: WING CLAP: Wings physically struck together above the body at top of upstroke. Leading edges make contact. Air compression creates pressure wave. Sharp percussive crack.
song_metrics.note_types: Wing clap (primary, display/alarm -- mechanical), coo-roo (secondary, territorial/courtship -- vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Display flight: 3-8 claps during climbing phase, then stiff-wing V glide. Alarm: single explosive burst from flock flush.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, percussive, startling. Not tonal. A physical impact sound. Completely different character from all Columbidae vocalizations.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Louder than any Columbidae vocalization. Individual clap: audible 50-150m. Flock takeoff: audible 100-300m. Breaks the E=3 family baseline.
song_metrics.tempo: Display: 3-8 claps in rapid succession during climbing flight. Alarm: instantaneous burst.
song_metrics.context: Male display flights: climbing with claps, then V-glide. Alarm: flock flush from ledge. Both year-round.
song_metrics.key_property: WING CLAP -- broadband 200-5000 Hz, percussive, wings slapped above body. E=4. FOURTH AND FINAL COLUMBIDAE. THIRD MECHANICAL SOUND (after Goldeneye 124, Mourning Dove 150). Breaks E=3 family baseline. Feral. Urban cliff adaptation. 260M global. Domesticated 5,000-10,000 years ago. Quartet complete.

plumage.overall: Wild-type: blue-gray overall with iridescent green-purple neck, two black wing bars, white rump. Feral populations show extreme variation: white, piebald, rusty, dark, checkered. Centuries of selective breeding produced diverse morphs.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Highly variable in feral populations. Wild-type: blue-gray with two wing bars. The most morphologically variable bird in any city.
plumage.flight_pattern: Strong, direct. Display flight: steep climbing with exaggerated wingbeats and claps, then stiff V-wing glide. Flock takeoff: explosive burst.

physical_description.overall: Length: 29-37 cm. Weight: 240-380 g. Medium-large pigeon. Smaller than Band-tailed (300-450g). Larger than Mourning Dove (100-170g). Similar size to Collared-Dove (150-240g) but heavier.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Urban habitat. Variable plumage. Two wing bars in wild-type. Iridescent neck. Display flight with wing claps.
physical_description.field_id: The city pigeon. On ledges, bridges, sidewalks, parking garages. Listen for the sharp wing clap during display flights or alarm takeoff. Extremely variable in color.

nodes.title: Wing Clap -- Percussion, Not Feather
nodes.description: Wings physically struck together above the body. Leading edges contact at top of upstroke. Air compression creates pressure wave. Fundamentally different from Goldeneye/Mourning Dove feather whistles. Percussion vs. aeroelastic vibration.

nodes.title: Mechanical Sound Trilogy Complete
nodes.description: Three mechanical sounds: Goldeneye wing whistle (124, E=5), Mourning Dove wing whistle (150, E=3), Rock Pigeon wing clap (152, E=4). Progression from feather-generated to body-generated. Whistle to clap.

nodes.title: Columbidae Quartet Complete (149-152)
nodes.description: Four degrees, four species: native forest (149), native open (150), invasive (151), feral mechanical (152). Native-to-non-native. Vocal-to-mechanical. E=3, E=3, E=3, E=4. Habitat: forest > open > urban > urban cliff. The displacement story told in four degrees.

nodes.title: Cliff to City -- Urban Adaptation
nodes.description: Wild Rock Pigeons nest on Mediterranean sea cliffs. Domesticated 5,000-10,000 years ago. Transported globally. Feral populations recolonized buildings as cliff substitutes. The urban environment is an accidental recreation of ancestral habitat. 260M global.

cross_references.relationship: MECHANICAL SOUND PAIR! Both Columbidae with mechanical sounds. Mourning Dove: feather whistle (modified primary). Rock Pigeon: wing clap (body contact). Different mechanisms.
MECHANICAL SOUND TRILOGY! Goldeneye wing whistle (124), Mourning Dove wing whistle (150), Rock Pigeon wing clap (152). Three mechanical sounds. Feather to body.
NATIVE vs. FERAL BOOKENDS! Band-tailed (native, forest, declining, 149) opens the quartet. Rock Pigeon (feral, urban, 260M, 152) closes it. The displacement arc.
NON-NATIVE PAIR! Both non-native urban Columbidae. Collared-Dove (invasive, vocal, 30 years) and Rock Pigeon (feral, mechanical, centuries). Different timescales.
